focus_areas: with() scope escape in _.template() default mode
Type coercion attacks in comparison functions

findings.title: Arbitrary code execution via _.template() with().default mode escape
findings.description: When _.template() is called without an explicit `variable` setting, line 77 wraps generated source in `with(obj||{}){...}` . This creates a with-statement that grants the template body access to ALL properties of the global scope chain as implicit variables. An attacker controlling the template string can escape the with-block scope by referencing Object.prototype properties (e.g., `constructor`, `__proto__`, `prototype`) to access the global object and execute arbitrary code. CVE-2021-23358 partially mitigated this by adding a bareIdentifier check on the `variable` parameter, but the `with(obj||{})` wrapper itself remains the default when no variable is specified (line 75-78). The attack path: pass untrusted template string → template compiler generates `with(obj){ __t=(constructor) }` → access `constructor.constructor('return this')().process.exit()` → full RCE in Node.js or sandbox escape in browser.
findings.exploit_scenario: In a Node.js app: `_.template(userControlledString)()` where userControlledString contains `constructor.constructor('require("child_process").execSync("id")')`. Without explicit `variable` option, the with(obj) scope grants access to Object.prototype.constructor which can reach the global object.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `with(obj||{})` default (line 77) entirely. Require callers to explicitly set the `variable` option to a validated identifier. Throw by default when `variable` is unspecified rather than falling back to with().
findings.confidence_reason: Well-documented attack class (CVE-2021-23358). The with() pattern on line 77 is confirmed present in code. The bareIdentifier check only guards the variable name, not the with() construct itself. High confidence this remains exploitable when variable is unspecified.

findings.title: Unvalidated template interpolation produces arbitrary code via Function constructor
findings.description:
Line 87 uses `new Function(argument, '_', source)` to compile template output. The `source` variable is constructed from untrusted template input: interpolate expressions are embedded directly as JavaScript expressions (line 59: `'+'
((__t=(' + interpolate + '))==null?'':__t)+'
'`). If the template string is attacker-controlled, any valid JavaScript expression can be evaluated in the compiled function scope. The bareIdentifier check on line 72 only validates the `variable` name, not the template content itself. Attack path: attacker provides `_<%=evil%>` template → source becomes `'+'
((__t=(evil))==null?'':__t)+'
'` → new Function evaluates `evil` expression → arbitrary code execution.
findings.exploit_scenario: User submits template string `_-_<%=require('fs').readFileSync('/etc/passwd')%>_` in a server-side Node.js app. The interpolated expression is directly embedded into the Function constructor body and executed.
findings.recommendation: Only allow safe interpolation syntax (e.g., variable names or property access chains, not arbitrary expressions). Alternatively, use a template engine that sanitizes expressions or compiles to a non-executable AST representation.
findings.confidence_reason: The Function constructor on line 87 is clearly visible. Interpolation expressions (line 59) are embedded raw without sanitization. CVE-2021-23358 confirms this class. The check on line 72 only guards the variable setting name, not the template content. Very solid finding.

findings.title: Prototype pollution via _.extend() through inherited property enumeration
findings.description: _.extend() delegates to createAssigner(allKeys) where allKeys (modules/allKeys.js:6-12) uses `for...in` enumeration which traverses the prototype chain including inherited properties like `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype`. When allKeys encounters a property from Object.prototype, it returns that key (e.g., '__proto__') which is then used as an assignment target in createAssigner.js:13: `obj[key] = source[key]`. In environments where `__proto__` assignment works (Node.js, most browsers), this directly pollutes Object.prototype. Additionally, collectNonEnumProps (modules/_collectNonEnumProps.js:36) can enumerate `constructor` and `prototype` properties on the source object, providing another path. The assigner has no guard against `__proto__`, `constructor`, and `prototype` keys.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker controls input object: `{__proto__: {isAdmin: true}}`. Passing this to `_.extend({}, maliciousInput)` causes all subsequent object literals to inherit `isAdmin: true`. Or: `_.defaults(obj, {constructor: {prototype: {admin: true}}})` in environments supporting constructor-prototype pollution path.
findings.recommendation: Filter out prototype-inherited keys in allKeys() or createAssigner: explicitly skip '__proto__', 'constructor', 'prototype'. Or use `Object.defineProperty` with `enumerable: false` to prevent injection. Consider requiring objects created with `Object.create(null)` as sources.
findings.confidence_reason: The for...in → allKeys → createAssigner chain on line 12 is confirmed. The THREAT_MODEL.md lists T3 as 'unmitigated'. In most JS engines, `__proto__` assignment via bracket notation works regardless of enumerable status. This is a real prototype pollution vector.

findings.title: Prototype pollution via _.defaults() constructor injection path
findings.description: _.defaults() delegates to createAssigner(allKeys, true) — same allKeys enumeration as extend but with defaults=true (only sets if property is undefined). Since defaults only writes missing properties, attacker must first ensure target has no own `__proto__` property, then inject via `__proto__` key from the source. The allKeys() function traverses the prototype chain, so `source.constructor` yields the Object constructor function, and `source.constructor.prototype` is accessible via the chain. In `_.defaults`, if obj.__proto__ is undefined (which it always is for plain objects), then obj['__proto__'] = source['__proto__'] fires, polluting Object.prototype.
findings.exploit_scenario: Given a plain object `target = {}`, calling `_.defaults(target, {constructor: {prototype: {pwned: true}}})` will set Object.prototype.pwned to true because createAssigner iterates inherited properties via allKeys() and assigns them when the target lacks the own property.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit denial of 'constructor' and 'prototype' keys in createAssigner. Use Object.defineProperty or Object.create(null) guard. Deprecate _.defaults in favor of Object.assign with pre-filtered sources.
findings.confidence_reason: The defaults module calls createAssigner(allKeys, true) — same code path as extend. The allKeys traversal picks up constructor/prototype from Object.prototype. This is the constructor.prototype path of prototype pollution. Confirmed by code analysis.

findings.title: Unrestricted function injection via _.mixin() pollutes global namespace
findings.description: _.mixin() (line 9) accepts any object and injects all function-valued properties onto both `_[name]` and `_.prototype[name]` without any allowlist or validation. An attacker controlling the argument to mixin() can add arbitrary functions (e.g., `eval`, `exec`, or attacker-defined functions) as methods on the Underscore prototype, making them available chainably on all wrapped values. The functions() utility (line 9) extracts all enumerable function properties from the input object without filtering. This effectively allows namespace hijacking and can bypass other security controls that expect standard Underscore functions.
findings.exploit_scenario: Attacker-supplied object passed to `_.mixin()`: `{eval: function(x){return require('child_process').execSync(x)}}` . This injects the function as `_.eval` and `_.prototype.eval`, making it available on every chained _.wrapped value. If the consuming application uses `_.tap` or similar on user-controlled data, this becomes exploitable.
findings.recommendation: Add a strict allowlist of permitted function names. Reject or log-warning on unknown function injections. Do not inject onto _.prototype without explicit opt-in.
findings.confidence_reason: The mixin code (line 9-15) clearly iterates all functions from input without any filtering. THREAT_MODEL.md lists T10 as 'unmitigated'. The injection affects both namespace and prototype, which is a significant attack surface expansion.

findings.title: Arbitrary method invocation via _.result() with attacker-controlled path
findings.description: _.result() (line 19) invokes `prop.call(obj)` whenever a traversed property is a function. With an attacker-controlled path traversing an object with attacker-tainted prototype, this can invoke hidden prototype methods. If the path includes keys like `constructor`, `toString`, `valueOf`, or `hasOwnProperty` that point to Function objects, those functions get called as methods of the traversal context. For example, a path `['constructor', 'constructor']` on any object resolves to `Object.constructor.constructor` (the Function constructor), and `prop.call(obj)` would invoke it.
findings.exploit_scenario: With an object that has an attacker-controlled prototype chain, `_.result(obj, ['constructor', 'constructor', 'return', 'process'])` followed by a subsequent lookup of the returned value as a function could lead to arbitrary evaluation. The real risk is when obj is untrusted and contains function-valued properties in its prototype chain.
findings.recommendation: Add a mode to _.result() that skips __proto__ lookups and only traverses Object.create(null)-safe paths. Validate that resolved paths don't traverse Object.prototype methods.
findings.confidence_reason: Line 19 unconditionally calls prop.call() on any function-valued property found during traversal. The THREAT_MODEL.md lists T6 as 'unmitigated'. The function detection on line 19 uses isFunction() which can match both own properties and prototype properties depending on the object's type.

findings.title: Prototype pollution via _.invert() with __proto__ valued keys
findings.description: _.invert() (line 5-10) creates a new result object using `{}` literal (line 5) and assigns `result[obj[_keys[i]]] = _keys[i]`. If any object value is the string '__proto__', 'constructor', or 'prototype', these become own properties in the result that pollute Object.prototype when used in future object constructions. The `keys()` function from keys.js uses Object.keys() which only returns own properties, but if the source object was itself polluted or created via `__proto__` assignment, those keys become own enumerable properties and get inverted.
findings.exploit_scenario: If `obj = {a: '__proto__'}`, then `_.invert(obj)` produces `{__proto__: 'a'}`, which sets the prototype of the result to the prototype of the value (undefined/null by default). More critically, if `obj = {a: 'constructor'}`, then `_.invert(obj)` produces `{constructor: 'a'}`, polluting any object that uses result as a prototype.
findings.recommendation: Use `Object.create(null)` on line 5 to create a prototype-less result object, preventing prototype pollution. Check that inverted values don't contain `__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`.
findings.confidence_reason: The result object on line 5 uses a plain `{}` literal, not Object.create(null). This is a known pattern for prototype pollution. The THREAT_MODEL.md lists T7 as 'partially_mitigated' noting Object.create(null) is needed for full mitigation.

findings.title: Prototype pollution via _.object() with untrusted key-value pairs
findings.description: _.object() (lines 7-15) constructs an object using `{}` literal and assigns keys directly: `result[list[i]] = values[i]` (line 10) or `result[list[i][0]] = list[i][1]` (line 12). If the input pairs include an entry where the key is '__proto__', 'constructor', or 'prototype', Object.prototype gets polluted. The function accepts any list of pairs from untrusted input, and uses Object.keys() internally (via getLength → Object.keys) for length retrieval but doesn't filter keys before assignment.
findings.exploit_scenario: Calling `_.object([['a', 1], ['__proto__', {'pwned': true}]], [])` or `_.object([['a', 1], ['__proto__', {'pwned': true}]])` directly sets the prototype of the result object, and in some JS engines, can pollute Object.prototype.
findings.recommendation: Use `Object.create(null)` for the result object. Validate and reject keys that match `__proto__`, `constructor`, `prototype`.
findings.confidence_reason: The {} literal on line 7 with direct key assignment on lines 10/12 is the same pollution pattern as invert.js. Keys come from untrusted input. Object.keys() is used for length but not for filtering. Moderate confidence because prototype pollution via direct assignment depends on JS engine behavior.

findings.title: Stack overflow risk in _.flatten() via excessive implicit depth
findings.description: _.flatten() (modules/flatten.js:6) delegates to _flatten which uses an explicit stack for iterative traversal (line 12: `stack = []`) and depth limiting (line 8: `depth = Infinity` by default). While the CVE-2026-27601 fix converted to trampolining (line 13-34), the default depth of Infinity on line 8 means the stack can grow unbounded. Each nested array pushes a frame `{i, v}` onto the stack, consuming O(n) memory where n is total array depth. An attacker can supply deeply nested arrays that exhaust heap memory: `Array(depth).fill().reduce((a,v) => [a], [1])` creates exponential memory usage proportional to depth.
findings.exploit_scenario: Pass a deeply nested array structure (depth 100,000+) to `_.flatten()` from a user-upload endpoint in a Node.js server. The explicit stack grows unbounded, causing out-of-memory crash or severe performance degradation.
findings.recommendation: Add a configurable maximum depth with a default reasonable limit (e.g., 1000). Return an error when the depth limit is reached. The `stack.length >= depth` check on line 23 provides the mechanism — just set default depth to a finite value.
findings.confidence_reason: The code has depth limiting (line 23) but defaults depth to Infinity (line 8). The trampolining fix for CVE-2026-27601 addressed stack overflow but not heap exhaustion. Medium confidence: the explicit stack prevents call-stack overflow but heap exhaustion remains a valid DoS vector.

findings.title: Quadratic time complexity in _.isEqual() cycle detection via linear search
findings.description: _.isEqual() uses iterative trampolining (line 25) to avoid stack overflow from CVE-2026-27601, which is correct. However, cycle detection uses a linear search over aStack/bStack arrays (line 119-128: `while (length--)` searching `aStack[length] === a`). For objects with many unique nested structures, this linear search makes cycle detection O(n²) rather than O(n). An attacker can craft objects with deliberately distinct nested structures to force quadratic comparison time. The keys comparison on line 152 (`keys(b).length !== length`) also triggers a full keys enumeration on the second object even before deep comparison, adding to the complexity.
findings.exploit_scenario: Send two large objects with millions of unique nested nodes to `_.isEqual()` in a request-handling path. Each node gets pushed to the todo stack and checked against the full aStack via linear search, causing quadratic time blowup that saturates the CPU.
findings.recommendation: Replace linear search with a Set-based or Map-based lookup for cycle detection. Use WeakSet for automatic garbage collection of detected cycles. Fix line 152 to use a pre-computed key count or cache rather than re-enumerating keys(b).
findings.confidence_reason: The linear search pattern on lines 119-128 is clearly visible. For objects with many unique nested structures (no cycles), every structure must be compared against the entire aStack. Medium confidence because this requires a large number of unique nodes to be practically exploitable, and the iterative fix already eliminates the worst DoS vectors.
